walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Vegas Showdown set up. First, place the game board in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. This board has spaces for the three basic tile stacks — Restaurant, Lounge, and Slots — as well as three premier tile spaces with bidding tracks underneath them. You will also see spaces marked for Renovate and Publicity actions, and the Fame score track running around the edge.

walkthrough.title: Stack the Basic Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the Restaurant, Lounge, and Slots tiles. These are the basic tiles that are always available for bidding. Stack each type face up on its matching marked space on the game board. Important: if you are playing with only three players, remove three tiles from each of these stacks before you begin. Set those removed tiles back in the box.

walkthrough.title: Choose Colors and Take Components
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes the hotel-casino sheet for that color. You also grab the matching Population marker, Revenue marker, Fame marker, and Bid marker in your color. There are five color sets available, so set aside any unused ones.

walkthrough.title: Set Your Starting Tracks
walkthrough.text: On your hotel-casino sheet, you will see a track that runs along one edge with numbers for Population and Revenue. Place your Population marker on the 8 space and your Revenue marker on the 5 space. Then place your Fame marker on the 0 space of the score track on the main game board. Everyone starts with 8 Population, 5 Revenue, and 0 Fame.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Premier Tile Stacks
walkthrough.text: The remaining tiles are the premier tiles. These come in three sizes — small, medium, and large. Each tile has either an A back or a B back. For each size, separately shuffle the B tiles face down, then shuffle the A tiles face down, and place the A stack on top of the B stack. This ensures A tiles come out first. Place the three resulting stacks next to the game board. There are 26 small tiles, 20 medium tiles, and 17 large tiles total.

walkthrough.title: Reveal Starting Premier Tiles
walkthrough.text: Flip the top tile from each of the three premier stacks and place them on the three empty premier spaces on the game board. In a 3-player game, leave the space marked '4-5 Players Only' empty for the entire game. For each tile you placed, find its starting-bid price printed on the tile, then put a Minimum-Bid marker on the matching circular number on that tile's bidding track.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Reference Charts and Event Deck
walkthrough.text: Place the two Building Prerequisites reference charts near the game board where everyone can see them. These charts show which tiles you need before you can place a premier tile. Then shuffle the 33-card Event deck and place it face down next to the board. Events drive the game forward each turn by introducing new premier tiles and triggering special rules.

walkthrough.title: Assign the Banker and Distribute Money
walkthrough.text: Choose one player to be the banker. That player puts all 80 money chips within easy reach — these are 50 white chips worth $1 each, 25 red chips worth $5 each, and 5 green chips worth $25 each. This pile is the bank. The banker gives each player $20 worth of chips to start.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Randomly decide who goes first. Give that player the Turn Order Button. Play will proceed clockwise from this player. The Turn Order Button gets passed to the left at the end of each turn, so everyone eventually goes first. You are all set — time to start building your hotel-casino!

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Vegas Showdown is played in turns. Each turn follows six steps in order: 1) Drop Prices, 2) Flip New Tiles, 3) Collect Income, 4) Choose Actions, 5) Adjust Fame/Revenue/Population, and 6) Pass the Turn Order Button. The first player — whoever has the Turn Order Button — manages steps 1 and 2, then everyone participates in steps 3 through 6. Let's walk through each step.

walkthrough.title: Step 1: Drop Prices
walkthrough.text: Skip this on the very first turn of the game. On every other turn, the first player drops the minimum bid on each premier tile currently on the board. Move each Minimum-Bid marker down to the next lower circular space on the bidding track, skipping any square spaces. For example, a marker on 18 drops to 12, not 15. If a marker drops below the 3 space, remove that tile from the game entirely — nobody gets it. This creates urgency: tiles get cheaper each turn, but wait too long and they vanish.

walkthrough.title: Step 2: Flip New Tiles
walkthrough.text: If any premier spaces on the board are empty, you need to fill them. Draw one Event card from the deck and follow its instructions. Some events affect bidding, some give bonuses, and some restrict actions for the turn. If an Event says you cannot bid on a certain tile type, place a No-Bid marker on it as a reminder. After resolving the Event, check the symbol in the center of the card — it indicates which premier stack to draw from (small, medium, or large). Flip the top tile of that stack and place it on an empty premier space, setting its Minimum-Bid marker at the tile's starting-bid price. Repeat for each empty premier space. If a stack runs out and you cannot replace a tile, the game ends immediately.

walkthrough.title: Step 3: Collect Income
walkthrough.text: Now everyone gets paid. The banker gives each player money equal to the lesser of their Population and Revenue. At the start, that is the lesser of 8 and 5, so everyone collects $5. As you build your hotel-casino, both tracks go up, and your income grows. Your money is always public knowledge — everyone can see how much cash you have. Green chips are $25, red chips are $5, and white chips are $1.

walkthrough.title: Step 4: Choose Actions — Overview
walkthrough.text: Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player chooses one of three actions: Bid on a Tile, Renovate, or Publicity. Once you choose an action, you are locked in unless you get outbid on a tile. This is the heart of the game — you are competing with other players for limited construction resources while trying to build the most prestigious hotel-casino.

walkthrough.title: Action: Bidding on Tiles
walkthrough.text: To bid on a tile, place your Bid marker on the bidding-track space showing the amount you want to pay. For premier tiles, you must bid at or above the Minimum-Bid marker. For basic tiles (Restaurant, Lounge, Slots), you must bid at or above the yellow minimum-bid space. If someone else already has a bid on your tile, you must bid higher than them. You cannot bid more than your cash on hand, and you cannot exceed the highest space on the track. If you get outbid, you wait until your turn comes around again, pick up your Bid marker, and choose a new action — you can bid higher on the same tile, bid on a different tile, or take a completely different action. Bidding is complete once every player has an action and nobody has been outbid.

walkthrough.title: Purchasing and Placing Tiles
walkthrough.text: After all bidding is done, each player who won a tile pays the bank the amount they bid and takes the tile. You can place the tile immediately on your hotel-casino sheet, or set it aside for later placement. There is no limit to how many tiles you can hold off to the side. Placement rules matter though: yellow casino tiles must connect to your casino entrance via an uninterrupted path of tiles without crossing tile walls. Blue hotel tiles must connect to your hotel entrance the same way. Green tiles can connect to either entrance. Premier tiles have additional prerequisites — check the Building Prerequisites chart to see which tiles you need on your sheet before you can place a premier tile. For example, you need a Slots tile before you can place Fancy Slots.

walkthrough.title: Action: Renovate
walkthrough.text: Instead of bidding, you can place your Bid marker on the Renovate space. Any number of players can renovate on the same turn. When you renovate, you may remove 0, 1, or 2 tiles from your hotel-casino sheet, and then place 0, 1, or 2 tiles from your off-sheet supply onto your sheet. You do not have to remove the same number you place. All placement rules still apply when you are finished. This is incredibly useful for rearranging your layout to connect entrances, create diamond patterns, or make room for a key premier tile.

walkthrough.title: Action: Publicity
walkthrough.text: The third action option is Publicity. Place your Bid marker on the Publicity space — any number of players may choose this action on a given turn. When you take Publicity, you immediately gain 1 Fame, and you may also place 1 tile from your off-sheet supply onto your hotel-casino sheet, following normal placement rules. This is a nice option when you cannot afford to bid, or when you have a tile sitting on the side that you are finally ready to place.

walkthrough.title: Step 5: Adjust Fame, Revenue, and Population
walkthrough.text: After tiles are placed, update your tracks. For each newly placed tile, add its Fame, Revenue, and Population bonuses to the appropriate tracks. Fame is tracked on the game board, while Population and Revenue are tracked on your hotel-casino sheet. If you renovated and removed tiles, subtract those bonuses first before adding the new ones. Each player also retrieves their Bid marker at this point.

walkthrough.title: Step 6: Pass the Turn Order Button
walkthrough.text: The player with the Turn Order Button passes it to the player on their left. That player will be the first to act during the next turn. Also, remove any No-Bid markers that were placed during the Flip New Tiles step. Then start the next turn from Step 1.

walkthrough.title: Game End Conditions
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when either of two things happens: a player completely fills their hotel-casino sheet with tiles, or you try to replace a premier tile but no tiles remain in the stack indicated by the Event card. When either condition is met, the game stops and you move to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: At game end, players score Fame in several categories. You get 5 Fame for completely filling your casino section with yellow and/or green tiles. Another 5 Fame for completely filling your hotel section with blue and/or green tiles. 3 Fame if your casino entrance connects to your hotel entrance via an uninterrupted tile path. The player with the highest Population scores 5 Fame, second gets 3, third gets 1 — same for highest Revenue. You score 1 Fame for each full $10 in cash. You also score 1 Fame for each three-quarter diamond shape and 3 Fame for each full diamond shape formed by theme bonus symbols on your tiles.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The player with the most total Fame wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most leftover cash wins. If still tied, the game is a draw. When scoring ties for Population or Revenue rankings, tied players split the Fame for their place and the next place down, rounding fractions down. Two players tied for first each get 4 Fame instead of 5 and 3. Three players tied for first each get 3 Fame.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Strip
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first turn with three players — Red, Blue, and Green. Each player starts with $20, Population 8, Revenue 5, and Fame 0. The board has Restaurant, Lounge, and Slots stacks plus three premier tiles already revealed from setup. Follow along and you will see bidding wars, tile placement, and the key decisions that shape your hotel-casino.

walkthrough.title: Skip Drop Prices (First Turn)
walkthrough.text: Since this is the very first turn of the game, we skip Step 1 entirely. Premier tile prices stay at their starting-bid values. Remember, you only drop prices starting from the second turn onward.

walkthrough.title: Flip New Tiles (First Turn)
walkthrough.text: All three premier spaces are already filled from setup, so there are no empty spaces to fill. We skip the Event card draw this turn. In future turns, whenever a premier tile is purchased or removed, you will draw an Event card to fill the gap.

walkthrough.title: Collect Income
walkthrough.text: Time to get paid. Every player has Population 8 and Revenue 5, so everyone collects the lesser of those two — $5 from the bank. Each player now has $25 total ($20 starting plus $5 income). As your Population and Revenue grow throughout the game, this income increases, which is crucial for winning bidding wars later.

walkthrough.title: Red Bids on Restaurant
walkthrough.text: Red has the Turn Order Button and goes first. Red decides to bid on a Restaurant tile. The yellow minimum-bid price for Restaurant is $5. Red places their Bid marker on the 5 space of the Restaurant bidding track. A Restaurant costs $5 and gives 1 Population — a solid opening move to boost income.

walkthrough.title: Blue Bids on the Same Restaurant
walkthrough.text: Blue also wants a Restaurant. But Red already has a bid of $5 on it, so Blue must bid higher. Blue places their Bid marker on the 8 space. Now Red has been outbid. Red will get another chance to act when their turn comes around again.

walkthrough.title: Green Bids on Lounge
walkthrough.text: Green goes for a Lounge tile instead, avoiding the bidding war. The minimum bid for Lounge is $5. Green places their Bid marker on the 5 space. A Lounge gives 1 Revenue, which will help increase Green's income on future turns.

walkthrough.title: Red Gets Outbid — Choosing a New Action
walkthrough.text: Red was outbid by Blue on the Restaurant. It is Red's turn again, and Red picks up their Bid marker and must choose an action. Red has three choices: bid higher on the Restaurant (say $10), bid on a different tile, or take the Renovate or Publicity action. Red decides to bid on a different Restaurant tile at $5, since there are multiple Restaurant tiles in the stack. Wait — actually, there is only one bidding track per basic tile type, so Red cannot bid on a second Restaurant while Blue is on that track. Red decides to outbid Blue instead, placing their marker on $10.

walkthrough.title: Blue Gets Outbid — The Bidding War Heats Up
walkthrough.text: Now Blue has been outbid. On Blue's next turn, Blue picks up their Bid marker. Blue could bid $12 or higher to take the Restaurant back, but that is getting expensive. Blue decides to cut their losses and bid on a Lounge instead. But Green already has a $5 bid on Lounge. Blue bids $8 on the Lounge, outbidding Green.

walkthrough.title: Green Gets Outbid — Pivoting
walkthrough.text: Green has been outbid on the Lounge. On Green's next turn, Green could bid higher on the Lounge, but instead Green decides to take the Publicity action. Green places their Bid marker on the Publicity space. Green immediately gains 1 Fame. Not a bad consolation — sometimes the smart play is to avoid an expensive bidding war and take a guaranteed benefit.

walkthrough.title: Bidding Complete — Purchase Tiles
walkthrough.text: Everyone has an action and nobody is outbid, so bidding is complete. Red pays $10 to the bank and takes a Restaurant tile. Blue pays $8 and takes a Lounge tile. Green already resolved their Publicity action. Red and Blue each place their tiles on their hotel-casino sheets. Red places the Restaurant connected to their casino entrance, and Blue places the Lounge connected to their hotel entrance.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake new players make all the time. Let's say Red tries to place the Restaurant tile somewhere on their sheet that is not connected to the casino entrance by a continuous path of tiles. That is not allowed. Yellow tiles must always be reachable from the yellow casino entrance without crossing any tile walls. If Red cannot place it legally, they have to set it off to the side and place it later via Renovate or Publicity. Always trace a path from your entrance to the tile before locking it in.

walkthrough.title: Adjust Fame, Revenue, and Population
walkthrough.text: Now update the tracks. Red placed a Restaurant, which gives +1 Population. Red moves their Population marker from 8 to 9. Blue placed a Lounge, which gives +1 Revenue. Blue moves their Revenue marker from 5 to 6. Green took Publicity and gained 1 Fame, which was already tracked. Each player retrieves their Bid marker.

walkthrough.title: Pass the Turn Order Button
walkthrough.text: Red passes the Turn Order Button to Blue, who is on Red's left. Blue will go first next turn. Remove any No-Bid markers (there are none this turn). That is the end of Turn 1!

walkthrough.title: Turn 2 Begins: Drop Prices
walkthrough.text: Now it is Turn 2, and Blue is the first player. The first thing Blue does is drop prices on all premier tiles on the board. Each Minimum-Bid marker moves down to the next lower circular space. If a premier tile started at 18, its marker now drops to 12. Tiles that were already cheap get even cheaper — or vanish if they drop below 3.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Flip New Tiles
walkthrough.text: No premier tiles were purchased last turn, so all spaces are still filled. We skip drawing Event cards. But here is an important preview: once someone buys a premier tile, the empty space will need to be filled. That is when you draw an Event card, check its symbol, and flip a new tile from the matching stack. Events keep the game dynamic and unpredictable.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Collect Income
walkthrough.text: Everyone collects income. Red has Population 9 and Revenue 5, so Red collects $5 (the lesser). Blue has Population 8 and Revenue 6, so Blue collects $6. Green has Population 8 and Revenue 5, so Green collects $5. Notice how Blue's Lounge purchase already pays off — $1 more income every single turn for the rest of the game. That adds up fast.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring Revenue and Population Balance
walkthrough.text: Here is another common trap. Your income equals the lesser of Population and Revenue. If your Population is 12 but Revenue is only 5, you only collect $5. It does not matter how high one track goes if the other lags behind. You need to build both tracks relatively evenly to maximize your income. Think of Population as how many customers you can serve and Revenue as how much money each customer brings in — you need both.

walkthrough.title: Blue Bids on a Premier Tile
walkthrough.text: Blue decides to go for a premier tile this turn. Let's say there is a Fancy Restaurant on the board with a current minimum bid of 12. Blue has enough cash and places their Bid marker on the 12 space. Premier tiles are more expensive but give bigger bonuses to Fame, Revenue, and Population. Plus they can unlock even higher-tier tiles on the prerequisites chain.

walkthrough.title: Prerequisites Check
walkthrough.text: But wait — can Blue actually place the Fancy Restaurant? Blue needs to check the Building Prerequisites chart. A Fancy Restaurant requires a Restaurant tile already on your sheet. Blue does not have one — Blue bought a Lounge, not a Restaurant. Blue can still bid on and buy the Fancy Restaurant, but will have to set it aside until they get a Restaurant placed first. This is a key strategic consideration: do not buy tiles you cannot use yet unless you have a clear plan to meet the prerequisites soon.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Buying Tiles You Cannot Place
walkthrough.text: This brings us to mistake number three. Buying premier tiles without having the prerequisites is tempting, especially when the price drops low. But that tile sits on the side earning you nothing — no Fame, no Revenue, no Population — while your opponents are placing tiles and building their engine. Unless the tile is about to be removed from the board (price below 3 next turn) and you can meet the prerequisite soon, it is often better to buy a basic tile you can place immediately.

walkthrough.title: The Night Club Exception
walkthrough.text: One special placement rule worth mentioning: Night Club tiles have extra requirements beyond the standard prerequisites chart. A Night Club must be placed so that one of its entrances connects directly to a Lounge or Fancy Lounge entrance. This makes Night Clubs trickier to place and means you need to plan your layout carefully if you want one. It is a good example of why the Renovate action exists — sometimes you need to rearrange to make room.

walkthrough.title: The 5-Player Slots Rule
walkthrough.text: Quick note for larger groups: in a 5-player game, there is a second bidding track for Slots. The two Slots tracks are independent, so two different players can purchase a Slots tile on the same turn. This helps prevent too much congestion in a bigger game. In 3 or 4 player games, only one Slots track is used.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen a complete turn, experienced a bidding war, learned about placement rules and prerequisites, and watched three common mistakes play out. The core loop of Vegas Showdown is simple — earn money, bid on tiles, build your hotel-casino — but the interaction between players, the Event cards, and the spatial puzzle on your sheet keeps every game different. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Roll the Dice
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things to keep in mind.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on balancing Population and Revenue, when to bid aggressively, and when to back off.

Second — reference the Appendix for every tile type, Event card, and scoring category explained.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building your empire!

summary.title: Before You Roll the Dice
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for tile types and scoring categories
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime via the Q&A tab

walkthrough.title: Balance Population and Revenue
walkthrough.text: This is the single most important tip in Vegas Showdown. Your income each turn equals the lesser of your Population and Revenue. If one track is at 12 and the other is at 5, you are only earning $5. Every tile you buy should be evaluated partly on which track it boosts. Restaurants give Population while Lounges give Revenue, so alternate between them to keep both tracks climbing together. A player earning $10 per turn will massively outpace someone stuck at $5.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Walk Away from a Bid
walkthrough.text: Bidding wars are exciting but dangerous. Every dollar you overpay for a tile is a dollar you cannot spend later. If a basic Restaurant is worth $5 and you end up paying $15 because of a bidding war, you have effectively burned $10. Watch your opponents' cash and their board state. If someone desperately needs a tile to complete their casino section, they will bid high — let them. Pivot to a different tile or take Publicity instead. The best deals come from tiles nobody else wants.

walkthrough.title: Invest in Income Early
walkthrough.text: Early turns should focus on boosting your income by buying tiles that increase Population and Revenue. A $5 tile that raises your income by $1 per turn pays for itself in five turns. Over a 12-15 turn game, that is $7-10 in profit. Meanwhile, a flashy premier tile that gives 3 Fame but no income boost might seem exciting, but it does not help you afford the next tile. Build your economic engine first, then spend your riches on high-Fame tiles later.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Layout from the Start
walkthrough.text: Your hotel-casino sheet is a spatial puzzle. Before you place your first tile, think about how you want to fill both sections and eventually connect your casino entrance to your hotel entrance. The 3-Fame bonus for a connected hotel-casino is significant, and the 5-Fame bonuses for filling each section are even bigger. Place tiles with an eye toward these end-game bonuses from the very beginning. It is much harder to rearrange later, even with Renovate.

walkthrough.title: Use Renovate Strategically
walkthrough.text: New players often overlook the Renovate action, but it can be game-changing. You can remove up to two tiles and place up to two tiles in a single Renovate action. This lets you rearrange your layout to connect entrances, create diamond patterns, or make room for a premier tile you have been holding. The best time to renovate is when you have tiles sitting off your sheet that would give you a big scoring bonus once placed properly. One well-timed Renovate can swing 5-10 Fame.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Event Deck
walkthrough.text: Event cards can restrict bidding, give bonuses, or change the game state in unexpected ways. Pay attention to what Events have already come out and what might be coming. If an Event puts a No-Bid marker on Restaurants for this turn, that is your chance to grab a Lounge unopposed while other players might be forced into suboptimal choices. Events also determine which premier tiles appear — understanding the deck helps you anticipate what is coming.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Forget the Diamond Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Theme bonus symbols appear on some tiles. When you place tiles so that these symbols align at intersections, you create partial or full diamond shapes worth 1 and 3 Fame respectively. These bonuses are easy to overlook but can add up to 6-10 extra Fame if you plan carefully. Check the symbols on tiles before placing them and try to align them when possible. Sometimes the difference between a good placement and a great one is rotating your mental plan to line up diamonds.

walkthrough.title: Keep Cash for the Endgame
walkthrough.text: At the end of the game, you score 1 Fame for every full $10 in cash. This means leftover money is never wasted. More importantly, cash is the tiebreaker if players end with the same Fame. In the late game, it can be worth skipping a marginal tile purchase and banking your cash instead. A player with $30 in cash at game end scores 3 extra Fame, which can easily be the difference between first and second place.

summary.title: Balance Population and Revenue
bullets: Income = lesser of Population and Revenue each turn
Alternate Restaurants (Pop) and Lounges (Rev) to keep both climbing
A player earning $10/turn massively outpaces one at $5/turn

summary.title: Know When to Walk Away from a Bid
bullets: Every dollar overpaid is a dollar lost for future turns
Watch opponents' cash and board state before escalating
Pivot to a different tile or take Publicity instead of overpaying

bullets: Buy tiles that boost Pop/Rev early — they pay dividends all game
$5 tile that raises income by $1/turn pays for itself in 5 turns
Build economic engine first, chase Fame later

Connected hotel-casino: 3 Fame; filled section: 5 Fame each
Much harder to rearrange later, even with Renovate

summary.title: Use Renovate Strategically
bullets: Remove up to 2 tiles, place up to 2 — powerful rearrangement tool
Best used when you have off-sheet tiles ready for a big scoring play
One well-timed Renovate can swing 5-10 Fame

summary.title: Watch the Event Deck
bullets: Events can restrict bidding or give bonuses — stay alert
No-Bid markers create opportunities for unopposed bids elsewhere
Events determine which premier tiles appear — anticipate the flow

summary.title: Do Not Forget the Diamond Bonuses
bullets: Theme bonus symbols at tile intersections form diamonds
Three-quarter diamond: 1 Fame; full diamond: 3 Fame
Can add 6-10 extra Fame with careful placement

summary.title: Keep Cash for the Endgame
bullets: 1 Fame per full $10 in cash at game end
Cash is also the tiebreaker for equal Fame
Sometimes banking cash beats buying a marginal tile

walkthrough.title: The Income Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The most reliable path to victory is building a strong income engine early. Focus your first three or four purchases on Restaurants and Lounges in roughly equal measure. Each Restaurant gives +1 Population and each Lounge gives +1 Revenue. If you can get both tracks to 10 or higher by mid-game, you are collecting $10+ per turn. That is double what most opponents earn, and it lets you comfortably win bidding wars for premier tiles in the second half of the game. Think of the early game as investing — you are building a money machine.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Bidding
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use bidding as a weapon, not just a purchasing mechanism. If you see an opponent is one tile away from filling their casino section (5 Fame bonus), you can bid on the tile they need — even if you do not want it. This forces them to overpay or miss out entirely. Similarly, if you have more cash than everyone else, you can make aggressive opening bids to scare opponents away from tiles you want. Just be careful not to bluff too hard — if nobody outbids you, you are stuck paying the full amount.

walkthrough.title: The Section Rush
walkthrough.text: Filling your casino or hotel section completely earns 5 Fame and also brings you closer to ending the game by filling your entire sheet. If you are ahead in Fame, rushing to fill your sheet ends the game before opponents can catch up. Focus on one section first — usually casino, since yellow tiles (Restaurant, Slots) tend to be cheaper and more plentiful. Once that section is filled, shift to the hotel section. A player who fills both sections collects 10 Fame in bonuses alone, which is often the margin of victory.

walkthrough.title: The Connection Play
walkthrough.text: Connecting your casino entrance to your hotel entrance scores 3 Fame. But the real value is in how it constrains your tile placement to be efficient. When you plan a connected layout from the start, you naturally fill more of your sheet and create more diamond opportunities. Green tiles are the key — they can connect to either entrance, making them the bridge between your casino and hotel sections. Prioritize green premier tiles that fit in the gap between your two sections.

walkthrough.title: Premier Tile Timing
walkthrough.text: Premier tiles drop in price every turn. The optimal buy point is usually two or three drops from the starting price — cheap enough to be a good deal but before the tile risks being removed at the 3-space threshold. Watching the price trajectory lets you plan your cash management. If a tile starts at 24 and drops to 18 then 12, buying at 12 saves you $12 compared to the first opportunity. But if three players all want it at 12, the bidding war might push it to 18 anyway. Read the table and pick your moment.

walkthrough.title: The Prerequisite Chain
walkthrough.text: Premier tiles follow a chain: basic tiles unlock tier-1 premiers, tier-1 unlock tier-2, and so on. The Dragon Room, for example, requires both Slots and Fancy Slots. Players who plan their prerequisite chain early can climb to the most powerful tiles while opponents scramble for basics. Check the Building Prerequisites chart at the start of the game and map out a path to the highest-tier tile you want. Then build backward — what do you need first? Buy those tiles even if they seem unglamorous.

walkthrough.title: Publicity as a Strategy
walkthrough.text: Publicity is often dismissed as the 'nothing else to do' action, but it has strategic uses. First, 1 guaranteed Fame per use adds up over multiple turns. If you take Publicity five times in a game, that is 5 Fame for free. Second, the ability to place a tile from your off-sheet supply is underrated. You can buy a tile you cannot place, hold it for several turns, then use Publicity to place it when you finally meet the prerequisites — and gain 1 Fame as a bonus. In tight games, a string of Publicity actions in the mid-game can put you ahead.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Cash Management
walkthrough.text: In the final two or three turns, shift your thinking to cash preservation. Every $10 in cash is 1 Fame, and cash is the tiebreaker. If you are already ahead in Fame, stop buying tiles and bank your income. If you are behind, calculate whether a tile purchase will earn you more Fame (from placement and bonuses) than the cash you spend would have scored. Often the math favors buying one more tile, but sometimes hoarding $20-30 in cash is the winning play. Count your opponents' potential final scores and play accordingly.

summary.title: The Income Engine Strategy
bullets: Buy Restaurants (+Pop) and Lounges (+Rev) in roughly equal measure early
Target both tracks at 10+ by mid-game for $10+/turn income
Double income = win bidding wars for premier tiles later

summary.title: Controlling the Bidding
bullets: Bid on tiles opponents need to force them to overpay or miss out
Aggressive opening bids scare opponents away — but you pay if unchallenged
Use cash advantage as leverage in bidding wars

summary.title: The Section Rush
bullets: Filling casino or hotel section = 5 Fame each
Casino usually easier to fill first (cheaper yellow tiles)
Filling your entire sheet ends the game — good if you are ahead

summary.title: The Connection Play
bullets: Connected entrance-to-entrance = 3 Fame
Green tiles connect to either entrance — use them as bridges
Planning connected layout also creates more diamond opportunities

summary.title: Premier Tile Timing
bullets: Tiles drop price every turn — buy 2-3 drops from starting price
Too early = overpaying; too late = removed from game or bidding war
Watch price trajectory and plan cash management around it

summary.title: The Prerequisite Chain
bullets: Map the prerequisite chain at game start: basic -> tier-1 -> tier-2
Example: Slots -> Fancy Slots -> Dragon Room
Buy unglamorous prerequisites early to unlock high-tier tiles later

summary.title: Publicity as a Strategy
bullets: 1 guaranteed Fame per use — 5 Publicity actions = 5 free Fame
Place off-sheet tiles via Publicity once prerequisites are met
Underrated in tight games — consistent Fame generation

Final 2-3 turns: consider banking income instead of buying tiles
Calculate whether a tile purchase earns more Fame than the cash it costs

entries.definition: A basic yellow (casino) tile available every turn. Gives +1 Population when placed. Must connect to your casino entrance. One of the cheapest and most important tiles for building income early.
A basic blue (hotel) tile available every turn. Gives +1 Revenue when placed. Must connect to your hotel entrance. Essential for balancing your income alongside Restaurants.

A basic yellow (casino) tile available every turn. Gives +1 Revenue when placed. Must connect to your casino entrance. Prerequisite for Fancy Slots and other higher-tier casino premier tiles. In a 5-player game, there are two independent Slots bidding tracks.

Special tiles that appear on the board via Event cards. They cost more than basic tiles but give larger Fame, Revenue, and Population bonuses. Each requires prerequisite tiles already on your sheet before placement. Come in three sizes: small, medium, and large.
The smallest premier tile size. There are 26 small tiles in the game, split between A-back and B-back stacks. A-back tiles are drawn first.
The mid-size premier tile. There are 20 medium tiles in the game, split between A-back and B-back stacks.
The largest premier tile size. There are 17 large tiles in the game, split between A-back and B-back stacks. Large tiles tend to have the biggest bonuses and highest costs.
Casino-side tiles. Must be placed so they connect to the yellow casino entrance via an uninterrupted path of tiles without crossing tile walls. Restaurant and Slots are basic yellow tiles.
Hotel-side tiles. Must be placed so they connect to the blue hotel entrance via an uninterrupted path of tiles without crossing tile walls. Lounge is a basic blue tile.

Dual-purpose tiles that can connect to either the casino entrance or the hotel entrance. Green tiles count toward filling either the casino or hotel section. They are key for connecting the two sides of your sheet.
A premier tile that requires a Restaurant already on your sheet. Provides larger Population and/or Fame bonuses than the basic Restaurant.
A premier tile that requires a Lounge already on your sheet. Provides larger Revenue and/or Fame bonuses than the basic Lounge.
A premier tile that requires a Slots tile already on your sheet. Prerequisite for the Dragon Room. Provides Revenue and Fame bonuses.
A high-tier premier tile that requires both Slots and Fancy Slots on your sheet. One of the most powerful tiles in the game with large Fame bonuses.

A premier tile with special placement rules. In addition to its standard prerequisites, a Night Club must be placed so that one of its entrances connects directly to a Lounge or Fancy Lounge entrance. Requires careful layout planning.

One of the two income tracks on your hotel-casino sheet, starting at 8. Represents the number of patrons your hotel-casino can accommodate. Increased primarily by placing Restaurant tiles. Your income each turn equals the lesser of Population and Revenue. Highest Population at game end scores 5/3/1 Fame.

One of the two income tracks on your hotel-casino sheet, starting at 5. Represents the money your hotel-casino can generate. Increased primarily by Lounge and Slots tiles. Your income each turn equals the lesser of Population and Revenue. Highest Revenue at game end scores 5/3/1 Fame.
The victory point track on the game board, starting at 0. Gained by placing tiles, taking Publicity, and end-game scoring bonuses. The player with the most Fame wins.

Money collected at the start of each turn, equal to the lesser of your Population and Revenue values. Starting income is $5 (min of 8 Pop, 5 Rev). Building both tracks evenly is essential for maximizing income.
The initial auction price printed on each premier tile. When a premier tile is placed on the board, a Minimum-Bid marker is set at this price on the tile's bidding track.
A marker placed on the bidding track to show the current minimum bid for a premier tile. Drops one circular space each turn. If it falls below the 3 space, the tile is removed from the game.

The numbered track beneath each tile on the game board. Contains circular spaces (used for minimum-bid drops) and square spaces (skipped during price drops). Players place their Bid markers on these tracks to indicate their bids.
When another player places a higher bid on the same tile you are bidding on. When outbid, you must wait for your turn to come around again, then pick up your Bid marker and choose a new action.

Placed on tiles or actions when an Event card restricts bidding for the current turn. Removed at the end of the turn during Step 6 (Pass the Turn Order Button). There are 6 No-Bid markers in the game.
A colored marker in each player's color used to indicate their chosen action — placed on a bidding track number, the Renovate space, or the Publicity space. Retrieved during Step 5 each turn.

One of three actions available each turn. Place your Bid marker on the Renovate space. Remove 0-2 tiles from your sheet and place 0-2 tiles from your off-sheet supply. Removed and placed counts need not match. All placement rules still apply when finished. Any number of players may renovate on the same turn.

One of three actions available each turn. Place your Bid marker on the Publicity space. Immediately gain 1 Fame and optionally place 1 tile from your off-sheet supply onto your sheet. Any number of players may take Publicity on the same turn.

The primary action in the game. Place your Bid marker on a bidding track at or above the minimum bid. If you win the tile, pay your bid to the bank and take the tile for placement or off-sheet storage.

One of 33 cards drawn when filling empty premier spaces. Each card has instructions (bonuses, restrictions, special rules) and a symbol indicating which premier tile stack to draw from. Some Event cards override general rules for the turn.

The 33-card deck of Event cards shuffled at setup and placed face down next to the board. Cards are drawn to fill empty premier spaces. When the deck runs out, reshuffle the discard pile. If a premier stack runs out when an Event card requires a draw, the game ends.

The requirement that certain tiles must already be on your hotel-casino sheet before you can place a premier tile. Shown on the two Building Prerequisites reference charts. Tiles and their prerequisites are connected by white lines on the chart.

Boundaries on tiles that block placement paths. When connecting tiles to an entrance, you cannot trace a path through a tile wall. This constraint makes layout planning important and sometimes necessitates Renovating.

The yellow entrance on your hotel-casino sheet. All yellow casino tiles must be connected to this entrance via an uninterrupted path of tiles without crossing tile walls. Green tiles may also connect here.

The blue entrance on your hotel-casino sheet. All blue hotel tiles must be connected to this entrance via an uninterrupted path of tiles without crossing tile walls. Green tiles may also connect here.

Tiles you have purchased but not yet placed on your hotel-casino sheet. There is no limit to how many tiles you can hold off-sheet. They can be placed later via Renovate, Publicity, or when purchasing another tile.
End-game scoring bonus. Score 5 Fame if the yellow shaded section of your hotel-casino sheet is completely filled with only yellow and/or green tiles.
End-game scoring bonus. Score 5 Fame if the blue shaded section of your hotel-casino sheet is completely filled with only blue and/or green tiles.
End-game scoring bonus. Score 3 Fame if you can trace an uninterrupted path of tiles from your casino entrance to your hotel entrance without passing through tile walls.

End-game scoring bonus. The player with the highest Population scores 5 Fame, second place scores 3 Fame, third place scores 1 Fame. Ties split the Fame for the tied place and the next place down, rounding fractions down.
End-game scoring bonus. Scored exactly like the Population bonus but based on Revenue track position. 1st: 5 Fame, 2nd: 3 Fame, 3rd: 1 Fame.
End-game scoring bonus. Score 1 Fame for each full $10 you have in cash at the end of the game. Keep any leftover amount for tiebreaking purposes.
Special symbols on certain tiles. When placed at tile intersections, they can form three-quarter diamond or full diamond shapes for bonus Fame at end of game.
A pattern formed by theme bonus symbols at tile intersections. Scores 1 Fame at end of game. Created when three of four possible symbol positions at an intersection are filled.
A pattern formed by theme bonus symbols at tile intersections. Scores 3 Fame at end of game. Created when all four symbol positions at an intersection are filled.

When players tie for Population or Revenue ranking, they split the Fame for the tied place and the next place down, rounding fractions down. Tied for 1st: 4 each. Tied for 2nd: 2 each. Tied for 3rd: 0 each. Three-way tie for 1st: 3 each.
Given to the first player at game start. Passed to the left at the end of each turn. The player holding it goes first during the Choose Actions step and manages Drop Prices and Flip New Tiles steps.

Each player's personal board showing the casino section (yellow shading), hotel section (blue shading), casino entrance, hotel entrance, and Population/Revenue tracks. Players place tiles on this sheet to build their hotel-casino.
80 chips total: 50 white ($1), 25 red ($5), 5 green ($25). Used for income and bidding. Your money is always public information. At game end, every full $10 scores 1 Fame.

Two reference charts showing which tiles are required before you can place each premier tile. Tiles and their prerequisites are connected by white lines. The number next to each premier tile indicates how many copies exist in the game.
The supply of money chips managed by the banker. Players pay the bank when purchasing tiles and receive money from the bank during income collection.

Premier tiles are divided into A-back and B-back stacks for each size. During setup, A tiles are stacked on top of B tiles so A tiles are drawn first. This ensures certain tiles appear earlier in the game.
A premier tile space on the game board that remains empty during 3-player games. In 4 and 5 player games, this space is used normally, providing an additional premier tile for bidding.
One of two game-end conditions. If any player completely fills their hotel-casino sheet with tiles, the game ends immediately and proceeds to final scoring.
One of two game-end conditions. If you try to replace a premier tile but no tiles remain in the indicated stack, the game ends immediately and proceeds to final scoring.
